mnemonics.title: Midpoint Circle P Update
mnemonics.content: P UPDATE Mnemonic: 'If Negative, add 2x+3; If Positive, add 2x-2y+5' - Think: N=add+add, P=add+sub+add (N=2x+3, P=2x-2y+5)

mnemonics.title: Ellipse Regions
mnemonics.content: Region 1: Slope > 1 (steeper than 45°) - move horizontally most often; Region 2: Slope < 1 (shallower than 45°) - move vertically more often

mnemonics.title: Circle Symmetry
mnemonics.content: 8-way symmetry: Think of a compass - N, S, E, W plus NE, NW, SE, SW - 8 points from center

acronyms.expansion: Midpoint Circle Algorithm - uses midpoint of two candidate pixels and circle equation to decide which pixel is closer to the true circle
Bresenham's Circle Algorithm - uses error term comparison to minimize distance between actual circle and chosen pixels
acronyms.usage: Popular in graphics libraries for line and circle drawing

acronyms.expansion: Decision parameter at step k in midpoint algorithm - determines which pixel to choose based on whether midpoint is inside or outside the circle
acronyms.usage: P < 0 means inside, choose upper pixel; P ≥ 0 means outside, choose lower pixel

visualTips.visualization: Imagine the circle passing through the midpoint between two candidate pixels. If the midpoint is 'inside' (toward center), choose outer pixel. If 'outside' (away from center), choose inner pixel.
Draw one octant (45° wedge), then mirror across both axes and the diagonal line y=x to get 8 octants - like folding paper 3 times to get 8 copies

The switch point between regions occurs where the ellipse slope equals 1 (45°). Before this, x increments each step; after this, y increments. The boundary is where dx/dy = 1 or 2x²b² = 2y²a² - 2ya² + b²

analogies.analogy: Drawing a circle on pixel grid is like marking dots on graph paper to approximate a compass-drawn circle - you choose the closest integer coordinates to the ideal mathematical circle.
It's like cutting a pizza into 8 equal slices - knowing one slice's toppings (pixels) tells you all 7 other slices automatically.
The decision parameter P is like a 'lie detector' - it tells whether the midpoint between two candidate pixels falls inside or outside the true circle, helping choose the better pixel.
